coredata.dc:title: A boost lubrication performance of reduced graphene oxide synergizing with carbonized polymer dots via tribochemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Guxia
Yang, Changxing
Li, Dan
Guo, Shengwei
coredata.dc:description: Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can become a new generation of lubricating materials because of its unique physicochemical properties. However, the aggregation phenomenon of rGO in base oils remains a significant bottleneck for its sustainable and industrial applications. To address this issue, we developed a strategy to enhance the lubricating properties of rGO using carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) assisted by the dispersant Span 80 (SP). This study demonstrated the effectiveness of this approach using various analytical techniques. Experimental data showed that the prepared CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited significant dispersion stability in sedimentation tests. From a macroscopic tribological viewpoint, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited excellent friction reduction and antiwear properties. Compared with the base oil, its average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter were reduced by 33.1 % and 39.8 %, respectively. Further experiments also revealed that the nanofluid exhibited efficient lubrication characteristics under certain loads, certain rotational speeds, and prolonged lubrication conditions. Mechanism analysis indicated that the interfacial interaction between CPDs and rGO produced a significant synergistic effect, which provided the conditions for the formation of a continuous and dense tribofilm, effectively isolating frictional mating surfaces, and achieving efficient lubrication. Therefore, this study presents a simple and universal solution to address the key issue of rGO in oil-based lubrication, laying the foundation for its large-scale industrial production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid
Dispersion
Tribology
Interfacial interactions
Tribofilm

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of CPDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by Key Research Project of North Minzu University (No. 2021KJCX03), Ningxia Key Research & Development Program (No. 2022BDE03003), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2023AAC03279), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52263021).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Process flow for the preparation of hybrid nanofluids.

Chemical structure and composition of CPDs. (a–c) TEM images (inset: particle size distribution of CPDs); (d) FTIR spectra; (e–i) XPS spectra, including the full spectrum as well as specific C 1s, N 1s, S 2p, and O 1s spectra.
Optical photographs of CPD/rGO, rGO/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids after standing for different periods.

Tribological properties of various nanofluids at different concentrations: (a, b) rGO/SP, (c, d) CPD/rGO/SP. (e, f) Comparison of the tribological properties of 15# white oil, SP, rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids.

Results of sliding wear tests using CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid under different applied load conditions: (a) friction coefficient–time curves, (b) average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter, and (c) optical microscopic images of the steel ball wear area.

Results of sliding wear tests conducted with CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid at different rotational speeds: (a) friction coefficient–time curves, (b) average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter, and (c) optical microscopic images of the steel ball wear area.

Three-dimensional surface morphology of steel ball wear areas lubricated with various nanofluids: (a) 15# white oil, (b) rGO/SP, (c) CPD/SP, and (d) CPD/rGO/SP. (e) Corresponding two-dimensional wear profiles.
(a–d) FE-SEM images, XPS (e) C 1s, (f) O 1s, (g) Fe 2p, (h) N 1s, and (i) S 2p spectra of wear regions obtained by 15# white oil, rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids lubrication, respectively.
Schematic diagram of the lubrication mechanism of CPD/SP, rGO/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids.
Comparison of the performance of this study with that of related lubricants reported in the literature.

head.ce:title: A boost lubrication performance of reduced graphene oxide synergizing with carbonized polymer dots via tribochemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can become a new generation of lubricating materials because of its unique physicochemical properties. However, the aggregation phenomenon of rGO in base oils remains a significant bottleneck for its sustainable and industrial applications. To address this issue, we developed a strategy to enhance the lubricating properties of rGO using carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) assisted by the dispersant Span 80 (SP). This study demonstrated the effectiveness of this approach using various analytical techniques. Experimental data showed that the prepared CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited significant dispersion stability in sedimentation tests. From a macroscopic tribological viewpoint, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited excellent friction reduction and antiwear properties. Compared with the base oil, its average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter were reduced by 33.1 % and 39.8 %, respectively. Further experiments also revealed that the nanofluid exhibited efficient lubrication characteristics under certain loads, certain rotational speeds, and prolonged lubrication conditions. Mechanism analysis indicated that the interfacial interaction between CPDs and rGO produced a significant synergistic effect, which provided the conditions for the formation of a continuous and dense tribofilm, effectively isolating frictional mating surfaces, and achieving efficient lubrication. Therefore, this study presents a simple and universal solution to address the key issue of rGO in oil-based lubrication, laying the foundation for its large-scale industrial production.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear phenomena are a primary issue in human development, causing energy dissipation and material loss in all mechanical systems with moving components [1–3]. On the basis of studies, it is estimated that this issue results in a third of the world's annual disposable energy consumption and economic losses ranging from 2 % to 7 % of the gross national product [4]. Advanced lubrication techniques are a means of realizing ultra-low friction and wear of materials [5,6]. Lubricants are particularly important because they act as “blood” for mechanical equipment, ensuring not only the reliable operation of the mechanical system but also significantly extending its service life [7]. In today's society, because of the continuous updates and iterations of mechanical equipment, more efficient, larger-scale, and intelligent equipment has emerged. Conventional lubrication methods, termed “simple lubrication”—primarily referring to conventional oils or greases without any special additives or techniques—can no longer meet the lubrication needs of emerging high-end devices [8,9]. Nanofluids are suspensions obtained by dispersing nanomaterials in a base fluid at a certain ratio [10]. Commonly used base fluids are typically water- or oil-based lubricants [11,12]. Owing to the Brownian motion of nanomaterials, the flow and heat transfer efficiency of nanofluids have been improved, thereby reducing the possibility of channel blockage [13,14]. These characteristics present nanofluids with outstanding application prospects in the field of high-performance lubrication. Span 80 (SP) plays a key role in nanofluids as a surfactant with a unique structure, including a hydrophilic head and hydrophobic tail [15]. The hydrophilic heads are adsorbed on the surface of the nanoparticles, and the hydrophobic tails point toward the oil phase [16]. Hence, SP forms a protective layer on the particle surface, effectively preventing particle aggregation and sedimentation through repulsive forces [17]. To solve the aforementioned problem, researchers have developed new nanomaterials, such as the graphene family of materials [18,19], boron nitride [20,21], and MXene [22,23], which are used as lubricating oil additives to improve the performance of the base oil. Among them, graphene is one of the liquid lubricants with the most promising prospects, gradually piquing intense interest in both scientific and industrial fields [24]. However, a major challenge in its use is the difficulty of dispersing it in base oil, which has become a bottleneck limiting its application [19]. Therefore, starting with graphene, the development of a simple, economical, and effective lubrication technology is crucial for advancing the research and development of high-performance finished oils.

To date, researchers have adopted the covalent grafting of surfactants and the preparation of composite materials to address the dispersion stability issue of reduced graphene oxide (rGO) [25]. However, these methods can adversely affect the pristine structure of rGO, thereby degrading its natural properties [1]. Nevertheless, carbon polymer dots (CPDs) prepared using a bottom-up synthesis method combine the multiple advantages of conventional carbon nanomaterials [26]. Because of their excellent properties, such as small scale, large specific surface area, designable structure, and excellent mechanical properties, CPDs have been used as new lubricant additives and have exhibited excellent tribological properties [9,11,27]. In addition, “tribochemistry” refers to chemical reactions instigated between nanofluids and the friction pair because of mechanical forces and frictional heat under boundary or mixed lubrication conditions [28]. These reactions form a protective film that can instantaneously compensate for the loss of the sliding interface, effectively reducing the friction coefficient and the wear rate of the materials and thus improving the durability and reliability of the mechanical system [27]. With these advantages, the spherical structure of CPDs can act as rolling bearings, increasing the contact area with the matrix and separating the two opposing surfaces, effectively converting sliding friction into rolling friction [29]. Therefore, CPDs are anticipated to be potential candidates for use as lubricant additives. In recent years, our research team has gained extensive experience in the application of functional materials [30–33]. By using tribochemistry, we have combined the advantages of functional materials with lubrication technology and designed efficient and sustainable lubricating oil additives. This study aims to meet the demands for various working conditions in future oil-based lubrication, an area that continues to be the focus of our research.

On the basis of the above background, we chose cost-effective o-phenylenediamine (o-PD) as the precursor and synthesized an amphiphilic CPDs using a bottom-up approach. We then proposed a strategy for enhancing the lubrication performance of rGO using dispersant-assisted CPDs and optimized the pairing law between CPDs and rGO to obtain a hybrid nanofluid that integrated friction reduction, antiwear, and repair functions. The nanofluid achieved both synergistic effects of sliding and rolling friction and had good dispersion stability. Next, by altering the concentration, load, rotational speed, and friction time, we conducted a systematic study on the tribological properties of the mixed nanofluid to determine its applicability across different working conditions. In addition, based on the results of a series of tribological comparative tests and analyses of wear surfaces, we further discussed the lubrication mechanisms of each component and the synergistic lubrication mechanisms between them. This study points in a viable direction for solving the critical issue of rGO in oil-based lubrication systems.

rGO (G600) was purchased from the Shanxi Institute of Coal Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, and its field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) image and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results are shown in Figs. S1 and S2, respectively. O-phenylenediamine (o-PD), sodium sulfate, and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. SP, which was commercially supplied by Shanghai Titan Technology Co., Ltd., China was used as a surfactant, primarily to aid dispersion. The 15# industrial white oil produced by Shandong Taichang Petrochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China, was selected as the base oil. To guarantee the reliability and accuracy of the results, the chemicals were used in their original state without any purification.

The process is depicted in Fig. 1 . A solution was prepared by dissolving 2.56 g of sodium sulfate in 20 mL of deionized water. After achieving complete dissolution, an equivalent volume of anhydrous ethanol was added to the solution. Subsequently, 0.43 g of o-PD were introduced into the harmonized solution and sonicated for 20 min to ensure complete dissolution. The mixture was then transported into an autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 9 h. At this stage, o-PD acts as a source of carbon and nitrogen, and undergoes pyrolysis and polymerization at high temperatures to form intermediate products rich in carbon and nitrogen. Sodium sulfate participated in the reaction as a source of sulfur. After the reaction, the temperature was moderated to ambient levels and the CPDs were acquired through a sequential process of ultrafiltration membrane filtration, dialysis, spin evaporation, and freeze-drying. In addition, under irradiation with an ultraviolet lamp (wavelength 365 nm), the prepared CPDs exhibited distinct yellow fluorescence.

Next, to prepare rGO-containing nanofluids, we selected different contents (0.01, 0.02, 0.03, and 0.04 wt%) of rGO and 0.6 wt% SP, which were added to the base oil. These mixtures were magnetically stirred for 30 min at 60 °C to obtain nanofluids containing rGO. After determining the optimal mass concentration of the rGO nanofluid, we further incorporated different concentrations of CPDs (0.02, 0.03, 0.04, 0.05, and 0.06 wt%) to investigate the synergistic lubrication effect between rGO and CPDs. In addition, we prepared a base fluid without any nanoparticles as a control group to compare and investigate the effects of different combinations of nanofluids on lubrication performance to determine the optimal nanofluid ratios.

Characterization of the morphology and particle size distribution of CPDs was performed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F). The chemical structure of CPDs was thoroughly examined and analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, WQF-520A) in conjunction with XPS (ESCALAB 250Xi+). In the examination of nanofluid phenomena, we used the sedimentation technique as a systematic approach to determine the stability of the dispersion. Further tribological tests were conducted using an MRS-10W four-ball friction tester. In this investigation, GCr15 standard steel spheres, each with a diameter of 12.7 mm, were chosen as the grinding medium. Throughout the sliding experiment, the test steel balls were fully immersed in the oil sample, and the friction coefficient curves of each nanofluid were recorded under constant conditions of 392 N, 1200 r/min, 75 °C, and 3600 s. Each test was repeated three times to ensure data reliability. Upon the completion of tribological examinations, a white light interferometer (WLI, Micro 1000) and FE-SEM (Sigma 500) were used to discuss the morphological characteristics of the wear region of the steel balls and to analyze the elemental distributions in the region via energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS). In addition, the chemical state and composition of the tribofilm formed by the nanofluids were analyzed by XPS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of CPDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and structure of CPDs were observed using TEM. As demonstrated in Fig. 2 a–c, these CPDs were uniformly distributed in discrete spherical structures, which provided superior lubrication conditions. By counting over 50 particles, we found that the distribution of particle sizes primarily fell within the 2–6 nm range, with an average size of 3.87 nm. Further analysis revealed that the lattice spacing of CPDs was 0.21 nm, revealing their highly crystalline structural characteristics and precisely matching with the (100) plane of graphite [34]. Accurate detection of the surface functional groups and chemical composition of CPDs was performed using FTIR and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2d, the sharp peaks that appeared in the range of 3160–3420 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, because of the action of intramolecular hydrogen bonds [35,36]. Within the 2900–3060 cm−1 range, the peaks were commonly thought to be the stretching vibrations of C–H, whereas the peak at 748 cm−1 signified the out-of-plane bending vibrations of the aromatic ring C–H [37,38]. Meanwhile, the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C=O/C=N were confirmed near 1550–1680 cm−1 [39,40]. Furthermore, the spectrum of CPDs revealed three significant absorption peaks at 1498, 1276, and 622 cm−1, each corresponding to the C=C, C–N, and C–S groups, respectively [11,39]. Note that the absorption peak of C=C specifically implied the presence of the sp2 C structure in CPDs, an observation that is consistent with the TEM analysis results. As seen in Fig. 2e–i, the XPS full spectrum analysis revealed specific signals of C, N, S, and O in CPDs, with their relative atomic contents being 29.07 %, 3.69 %, 12.65 %, and 54.59 %, respectively. The high-resolution C 1s spectra of CPDs were divided into four parts: namely C=C/C–C (284.8 eV), C–N/C–O (285.1 eV), C–S (285.9 eV), and C=O (288.6 eV) [36,41]. In particular, the chemical state dominated by the C=C/C–C bond implied a large and effective presence of sp2 C structures in CPDs, which was further confirmed by XPS. The N 1s analysis indicated the presence of pyrrolic nitrogen at 399.3 eV and graphitic nitrogen at 400.9 eV [37]. Correspondingly, the S 2P spectrum could be divided into peaks at 168.5 and 169.3 eV, representing the presence of S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2, respectively, demonstrating that the S element was successfully doped into CPDs [39]. In addition, the O 1s spectrum verified the existence of C=O (531.5 eV), S=O (531.8 eV), and C–O (532.5 eV) [18]. FTIR and XPS analyses indicated that the surface of the CPDs had a rich presence of oxygen-containing functional groups and hydrophobic carbon chains.

The long-term dispersion stability of nanofluids is a fundamental requirement for achieving efficient lubrication [42]. After ultrasound treatment, nanofluids containing rGO, CPD/rGO, rGO/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP were placed in an environment without external intervention. As depicted in Fig. 3 and S3, both CPD/rGO and rGO began the sedimentation process after 12 h. In the CPD/rGO nanofluid, over time, rGO and most CPDs gradually sedimented at the bottom of the glass bottle. As expected, some CPDs formed stable interactions with other components in the oil, thereby allowing these CPDs to remain dispersed. However, compared with the CPD/rGO nanofluid, the SP-assisted rGO and CPD/rGO nanofluid demonstrated favorable dispersion stability. This was due to the oxygen-containing functional groups in the structures of CPDs and rGO interacting with the hydrophilic heads of SP through hydrogen bonds, thereby facilitating the stable dispersion of the nanofluid. Interestingly, a stratification phenomenon appeared in the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid 10 days later. In this situation, due to the larger flake size and weaker interaction forces, rGO completely settled to the bottom, while CPDs remained stably dispersed. These results suggested that CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid possessed the potential to serve as lubricant additives.

To reveal the synergistic lubrication effects between CPDs and rGO, we evaluated the tribological properties of nanofluids containing SP, rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP. First, we evaluated the tribological properties of the rGO/SP nanofluid at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, when only pure 15# white oil was used as a lubricant, we observed that as the testing time progressed, the friction coefficient exhibited a gradual linear increase. At the same time, the average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter were also large. This was because the friction pair was under continuous operation, resulting in the lubricant oil film not being able to maintain a uniform distribution, which led to localized areas of oil film breakage, thereby exhibiting inferior tribological properties [43]. Compared with pure 15# white oil, the tribological properties of the rGO/SP-containing nanofluid were improved; the average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter exhibited a trend of first decreasing and then increasing. In other words, there was an optimal concentration (0.02 wt%). Second, to further evaluate the optimal ratio between CPDs and rGO, we conducted a study on the tribological properties of CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid. As listed in Fig. 4c and d, the addition of CPDs produced a friction coefficient curve that was lower than that of the rGO/SP nanofluid lubrication. This was attributed to the small size of CPDs, which allowed them to be embedded in the gaps generated by friction. This embedded capability endowed them with a self-repairing function [42]. Consequently, it aided the nanofluid in continuously forming a stable oil film between the contact surfaces, ensuring consistent lubrication. As expected, the average coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter of the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid also exhibited an initial decrease and then an increase and exhibited the best performance at 0.04 wt%. Finally, to illustrate the effects more clearly, we compared the performance of several nanofluids, including 0.6 wt% SP, 0.02 wt% rGO/SP, 0.04 wt% CPD/SP, and the mixed CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid. As presented in Fig. 4e and f, compared with the pure 15# white oil, the four nanofluids reduced the average friction coefficient by 16.6 %, 19.4 %, 26.1 %, and 33.1 %, respectively. They also reduced the wear scar diameter values by 25.9 %, 36.9 %, 36.1 %, and 39.8 %, respectively. According to these results, the last three nanofluids exhibited excellent results in friction reduction and antiwear, with the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid being particularly outstanding. This was attributed to the presence of SP, which ensured that CPDs and rGO nanoparticles were uniformly dispersed in the base oil, thereby guaranteeing a continuous supply of the additive on the contact surface, achieving a synergistic lubrication effect among multiple components.

The tribological properties of the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid prepared in this study and other lubricants reported in the literature are listed in Table 1 . For example, Man et al. [44] successfully and rapidly synthesized SSFCDs using a one-pot gas explosion technique using citric acid and urea. When used as an additive in polyvinyl alcohol, this resulted in reductions of 60.7 % and 19.7 % in the coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter, respectively. Lu et al. [45] synthesized CPs-OA with a particle size of 25–35 nm using a simplified one-pot pyrolysis technique. After amidation, covalent bonds were formed between OA and CPs-OA, resulting in excellent dispersion in PAO. The average coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter of PAO were reduced by 47 % and 30 %, respectively, when the additive concentration was 1.0 wt%. Similarly, Mungse et al. [19] successfully prepared alkylated rGO using a covalent grafting technique of OTES with rGO, which exhibited good dispersion in polyol lubricants. Specifically, the coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter were reduced by approximately 29 % and 18 %, respectively, when the concentration was 0.06 wt%. Turning to the study by Mao et al. [46] investigated the lubrication performance of three types of rGO with different micromorphologies. Among them, the rGO with regular edges exhibited the most outstanding lubrication performance, with the friction coefficient and wear scar depth decreasing by 27.9 % and 14.1 %, respectively. In addition, Zhang et al. [47] obtained graphene by liquid-phase exfoliation and successfully dispersed it as an additive in base oil after oleic acid modification. When the concentration of graphene was in the range of 0.02–0.06 wt%, the coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter of the lubricant were reduced by 17 % and 14 %, respectively. Overall, these nanoadditives exhibited significant effects at various concentrations, reduced the coefficient of friction by 17%–60.7 %, and decreased wear by 14%–70.7 %. More strikingly, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid prepared in this study exhibited superior performance at lower concentrations than many other reported lubricants. Hence, by leveraging the technical framework of this study and the experimental methods from the literature, future development of functionalized lubricants will be more efficient and controllable.

To gain a deeper understanding of the tribological properties of CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid under different applied loads and rotational speeds, we adjusted the load and rotational speed parameters, recorded variations in the friction coefficient over time, and quantified the average friction coefficient and wear scar diameter in various frictional environments. As displayed in Fig. 5 , with an increase in the applied load from 150 to 750 N, the average coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter decreased and then increased. Specifically, at a lower load (150 N), CPDs and rGO formed a stable nanolubrication film at the sliding contact interface. This oil film acted as a barrier, reducing direct contact between the substrate surfaces, thereby reducing the coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter. Furthermore, because of the high surface energy of CPDs, they are easily absorbed and distributed uniformly on the interface, thereby providing stability to the lubrication film. As the load increased to 300 N, the pressure at the sliding contact interface caused the nanoparticles to align more compactly, forming a more uniform lubricating layer and thus achieving improved lubrication. However, when the load was further increased to a critical point (close to 750 N), the lubrication film ruptured because of excessive compression, leading to direct contact at the friction interface, which would result in a rapid increase in the coefficient of friction and wear scar diameter [42]. As shown in Fig. 6 , both the friction coefficient and wear scar diameter exhibited a trend of decreasing first and then increasing when the tested rotational speed was within a specific range. Under lower speed conditions, there was some direct contact between the surfaces of the friction pair and the lubrication effect of the lubricating oil in these areas was not significant, which is typically referred to as mixed lubrication [51]. When the rotational speed reaches a certain value, the lubricant can more easily form a continuous oil film, resulting in the complete separation of the frictional pair surface and the realization of a fluid lubrication state [22]. When the rotational speed increased, the lubricant warmed up because of excessive shear speed, which decreased its viscosity. The oil film then thinned, triggering direct contact between the surfaces of the friction pair. Notably, at 1200 r/min, it precisely entered a favorable lubrication state, which resulted in a reduced contact area between the frictional mating surfaces, thereby minimizing the wear scar diameter. However, when the speed was increased to 1600 r/min, the lubrication state changed. At this point, the oil film became more stable, which led to a new low point in the coefficient of friction. In brief, CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid possessed efficient lubrication properties under certain load and rotational speed conditions.

In practical applications, the durability, stability, and efficient lubrication performance of CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid are key performance indicators [42]. As shown in Fig. S4, the coefficient of friction reached 0.054 at 50 min, after which a stable lubrication phase was entered. At 360 min, the friction coefficient dropped to 0.051 and the average wear scar diameter (0.58 mm) was 1.2 times that at 60 min. These data indicated that CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid were able to provide long-term stable lubrication.

To thoroughly investigate the antiwear performance of different nanofluids, we collected the three-dimensional surface morphology and two-dimensional profile of the wear region using WLI. As illustrated in Fig. 7 , for the wear surface lubricated by 15# white oil, we observed significant wear characteristics, which included large wear scar diameters and deep two-dimensional profiles. This phenomenon was attributed to the large amount of wear debris produced during the sliding wear process, which served as third-body wear particles, resulting in severe damage to the frictional surfaces [41,42]. As expected, the width and depth of the wear surface of the steel balls were significantly reduced, and the surface became smoother when lubricated with the rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids. Specifically, the steel ball lubricated with the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited the smallest wear scar diameter and the shallowest two-dimensional profile, which is highly consistent with the data shown in Fig. 4e and f. This effect was primarily due to the synergistic effect between CPDs and rGO and the formation of a stable lubrication film, which could significantly improve the performance of the wear surfaces [1].

To gain a deeper understanding of the lubrication mechanisms of the rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids, we further analyzed the corresponding wear surfaces using SEM, EDS, and XPS. As depicted in Fig. 8 a–d, the surface damage of 15# white oil lubrication was significant, including evident pits and grooves, corresponding to fatigue and abrasive wear [52]. In stark contrast, the wear surfaces lubricated by the rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids exhibited shallower grooves and were smoother. This outcome implied that during the friction process, these nanofluids effectively established a homogeneous and stable oil film, which protected the friction pair surfaces. In particular, the wear surface depth reduction resulting from CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid lubrication was the most pronounced, attributable to the improved wear resistance of rGO by CPDs. Moreover, comparing relevant EDS results (Figs. S5–S8), we identified the presence of N and S in the wear tracks of the CPD/SP and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids, attributable to the deposition of CPDs or the lubricating film formed by the friction-induced chemical reactions with the base surface [53]. In brief, the synergistic effect of CPDs and rGO made the oil film more solid and significantly facilitated friction reduction and antiwear properties. XPS analysis was then performed on the tribofilm formed on the wear surface. As seen in Fig. 8e–i, three distinct peaks were fitted in the C 1s spectrum at binding energies 284.8, 286.2, and 288.3 eV, corresponding to C–C, C–N/O, and C=N/O, respectively [52]. The characteristic peaks of the O 1s spectra at 533.3, 531.9, and 530.3 eV can be attributed to C=O, C–O, and Fe–O, respectively [54]. The appearance of Fe 2p at 710.2 and 712.2 eV indicated the formation of Fe2+ and Fe3+ on the wear surface, respectively, implying that the nanofluid chemically reacted with the substrate and oxygen in air during the friction process [55]. Notably, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid had the lowest ratio of Fe2+ to Fe3+ peak area in the wear region, suggesting that the nanofluid had more significant friction reduction and antiwear properties [56]. Furthermore, in the spectral analysis of N 1s and S 2p, the wear regions of the 15# white oil and rGO/SP nanofluids did not exhibit specific signals, whereas strong peaks could be fitted to the wear regions of the CPD/SP and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids. Specifically, the peaks of the N 1s spectra at approximately 399.1 and 400.2 eV can be attributed to C–N and N–H, respectively. The peaks located at 168.5 and 162.2 eV correspond to the S6+ valence state in FeSO4 and the S2− valence state in FeS, respectively [54,57,58]. Thus, the XPS analysis revealed that during the friction process, chemical reactions played a crucial role, and the lubricating film primarily comprised iron compounds, carbonitrides, and carbon. From these findings, the excellent tribological properties of the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid could be largely attributed to the synergistic effect between CPDs and rGO. This synergistic effect led to the formation of an efficient lubricating film, which was crucial for reducing friction and wear.

On the basis of the aforementioned analysis, we discussed the lubrication mechanisms of the CPD/SP, rGO/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids in depth. As shown in Fig. 9 , the combination of CPDs and rGO played a critical role in the formation of the boundary film, which was central to ensuring efficient lubrication. When lubricated with CPD/SP nanofluid alone, tiny CPDs could be embedded in tiny cracks or voids in the friction interface, effectively filling those voids, repairing surface defects, and thus forming a continuous and homogeneous deposition film [42]. At the microscopic level, CPDs functioned akin to “rolling balls” situated between two surfaces in friction, serving as a cushion for them, and preventing direct contact between the frictional pairs. Moreover, the free CPDs had a good polishing effect, providing additional protection for the friction interface. Conversely, when only the rGO/SP nanofluid was used for lubrication, because of external pressure and friction, the layered structure within rGO slid, resulting in a shearing effect [12,59]. Simultaneously, as the friction proceeded, rGO generated many wear fragments, which helped fill voids and repair damaged surfaces [60]. However, when both were used in tandem, they produced a significant synergistic effect through a series of frictional chemical reactions, including functions such as repairing, protecting, rolling, slipping, and polishing. More critically, SP as a dispersant could ensure the stable release of CPDs and rGO during the friction process to form a continuous and dense tribofilm, thereby reducing friction and wear. Because of these mechanisms, CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid particularly excel in tribology.

In this study, we successfully synthesized amphiphilic CPDs using a bottom-up approach. Next, with the assistance of SP, CPD/SP, rGO/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids were successfully prepared using a simple and novel method. A series of characterization techniques were employed to reveal the relevant tribological properties and lubrication mechanisms. The main conclusions were as follows: First, the oxygen-containing functional groups on the structures of CPDs and rGO interacted with the hydrophilic heads of SP through hydrogen bonds, effectively preventing their aggregation in 15# white oil. The CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid remained stable for approximately 10 days, suggesting that it had good dispersion stability. Second, the macroscopic tribological tests revealed that the rGO/SP, CPD/SP, and CPD/rGO/SP nanofluids could reduced the average friction coefficients by 19.4 %, 26.1 %, and 33.1 %, respectively. Their wear scar diameters were also reduced by 36.9 %, 36.1 %, and 39.8 %, respectively. Among them, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid exhibited the best friction reduction and antiwear performance. Third, under certain rotational speed and load conditions, it facilitates the formation of an oil film in the wear region, thereby reducing friction and wear. Fourth, the CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid provided long-term stable lubrication. Fifth, the analysis of wear surfaces emphasizes the critical role of a continuous, solid tribofilm on the tribological properties of CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid. Sixth, using widely accepted and cost-effective SP, we demonstrated the feasibility of enhancing the tribological properties of rGO with CPDs. We not only simplified the formulation process of additives and base oils but also provided practical solutions for the industrialization and commercialization of CPD/rGO/SP nanofluid.

Guxia Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Changxing Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Dan Li: Formal analysis, Suggestion. Shengwei Guo: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by Key Research Project of North Minzu University (No. 2021KJCX03), Ningxia Key Research & Development Program (No. 2022BDE03003), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningxia (No. 2023AAC03279), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52263021).

ce:section.ce:para:
